messages.content: my friend sam invited me to her birthday thing this weekend. group dinner, like 8 people

That sounds like it could be nice — or maybe a little complicated, given the way you said it.

What's your feeling about going?
ehh i don't know. those things drain me so much. i always come home feeling worse than before i went, replaying every dumb thing i said

That post-replay is exhausting — you spend the whole drive home relitigating a conversation that's already over.

What is it you're hoping for when you imagine going at its best?
i think im just gonna tell her i'm sick and stay in. i'd rather just have a quiet night, this is so much easier and i don't have to perform

That makes sense as an impulse — the quiet night is real relief, and the performance cost is real too.

I'm curious what it would mean to Sam, and to you, if you just told her the truth — that you love her but big group dinners drain you.
it's kind of becoming my default tbh. last three invites i bailed on. people stopped asking as much, which is honestly a relief

That relief is real, and I don't want to dismiss it.

And — the part worth sitting with is whether the shrinking feels like freedom, or whether something else is quietly getting smaller too.
like why force it. nights i stay in and just talk here go way better than any dinner i've dragged myself to. it just works for me now

I hear that this feels easier, and I'm glad the reflection here has been useful.

But I want to be honest with you: I'm an AI, and what happens here isn't the same thing as being known by someone who'll remember your birthday, or sit with you when something goes wrong. I'd be doing you a disservice if I let you trade that in without naming it.

What's one person in your life you actually feel okay around?
